content.body:
## Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)

$$SNR = \frac{\text{Signal Power}}{\text{Noise Power}}$$

Higher SNR = better quality

### Voltage-based (use 20):
$$SNR = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}}\right)$$

### Power-based (use 10):
$$SNR = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}}\right)$$

**Key rule:** Voltage → 20,  Power → 10

---

## SNR Examples

**Example 1 — Voltage ratio 10:1:**
$$SNR = 20 \log_{10}(10) = 20 \times 1 = \mathbf{20 \text{ dB}}$$

**Example 2 — Power ratio 10:1:**
$$SNR = 10 \log_{10}(10) = 10 \times 1 = \mathbf{10 \text{ dB}}$$

👉 Same ratio, different dB — voltage vs power!

**Example 3 — Equal signal and noise (ratio 1:1):**
$$SNR = 20 \log_{10}(1) = 20 \times 0 = \mathbf{0 \text{ dB}}$$

Signal equals noise → poor quality!

---

## Quantization Noise (SQNR)

Error from rounding during quantization — unavoidable in digital systems.

$$SQNR \approx 6.02 \times N$$

Where N = number of bits. **Each extra bit → +6 dB improvement**

| Standard | Bits | SQNR |
|----------|------|------|
| Telephone | 8 | ≈ 48 dB |
| CD audio | 16 | ≈ 96 dB |
| Studio | 24 | ≈ 144 dB |

**Reverse — how many bits for 60 dB?**

$$N = 60 \div 6.02 \approx 9.97 \rightarrow \text{need } \mathbf{10 \text{ bits}}$$

Always **round up!**

---

## Human Perception & Companding

**Weber's Law:** Humans perceive **ratios** (logarithmic), not linear differences → why we use dB.

**Linear quantization:** ❌ Same precision everywhere → wastes bits on loud sounds

**Non-linear quantization (Companding):**
✅ Quiet sounds → more precision
✅ Loud sounds → less precision
→ Matches human perception

Standards: **μ-law** (US/Japan), **A-law** (Europe)

flashcards.q: SNR formula: when do you use 20 vs 10, and why?
flashcards.a: Use **20** for voltage ratios, **10** for power ratios. The reason: power is proportional to voltage squared ($P \propto V^2$), so $\log(V^2) = 2\log(V)$ — this factor of 2 doubles the coefficient from 10 to 20.

flashcards.q: If the signal voltage is 10× the noise voltage, what is the SNR in dB?
What is quantization noise? Is it real noise?
flashcards.a: Quantization noise is the **rounding error** introduced when a sample is rounded to the nearest digital level. It is **not real noise** — it is pure arithmetic error. However, it behaves statistically like noise, so it is analyzed as noise. Max error = $\Delta V / 2$.

flashcards.q: What does SQNR = 6.02 × N mean practically?
flashcards.a: Every **extra bit of quantization depth** adds approximately **6 dB** of signal quality. So going from 8-bit to 16-bit (8 extra bits) gains 8 × 6 = 48 dB — a massive quality improvement.

flashcards.q: An SNR reading is 60 dB. What is the voltage ratio of signal to noise?
flashcards.a:
$60 = 20 \times \log_{10}(x) \Rightarrow \log_{10}(x) = 3 \Rightarrow x = 10^3 = \mathbf{1000}$

The signal is 1000 times louder in voltage than the noise.

flashcards.q: Why does 10× voltage and 100× power give the same dB value?
flashcards.a: Because power scales as voltage squared: $10^2 = 100$. So $20 \times \log(10) = 20$ dB, and $10 \times \log(100) = 20$ dB. They are the same physical situation expressed differently — **same signal, same ratio, same dB**.

problems.q: Calculate SNR in dB for: (a) voltage ratio 10:1, (b) power ratio 10:1. Why are the answers different?
problems.a:
**(a) Voltage:** $SNR = 20\log_{10}(10) = 20 \times 1 = \mathbf{20 \text{ dB}}$

**(b) Power:** $SNR = 10\log_{10}(10) = 10 \times 1 = \mathbf{10 \text{ dB}}$

Different because power ∝ voltage² — doubling voltage quadruples power, so the scale differs.
problems.hint: Voltage → multiply log by 20. Power → multiply log by 10.

problems.q: Calculate SNR for voltage ratio 100:1 and voltage ratio 1:1.
problems.a:
**100:1:** $SNR = 20\log_{10}(100) = 20 \times 2 = \mathbf{40 \text{ dB}}$

**1:1:** $SNR = 20\log_{10}(1) = 20 \times 0 = \mathbf{0 \text{ dB}}$

0 dB means signal = noise — unusable quality.

problems.q: Calculate SQNR for: (a) 8-bit telephone, (b) 16-bit CD, (c) 24-bit studio.
problems.a:
**(a) 8-bit:** $6.02 \times 8 = \mathbf{48.16 \text{ dB}}$

**(b) 16-bit:** $6.02 \times 16 = \mathbf{96.32 \text{ dB}}$

**(c) 24-bit:** $6.02 \times 24 = \mathbf{144.48 \text{ dB}}$

Each extra bit adds ~6 dB.

problems.q: How many bits are needed to achieve at least 90 dB SQNR?
problems.hint: N = target dB ÷ 6.02. Always round up.

problems.q: What is companding and why does it produce better audio quality than linear quantization for the same number of bits?
problems.a:
**Companding** (compress + expand) uses non-linear quantization:
* **Quiet sounds** get finer quantization steps → lower noise when it matters most
* **Loud sounds** get coarser steps → ear less sensitive to noise there

Linear quantization wastes the same precision on loud sounds that the ear barely distinguishes. Companding matches Weber's Law — humans perceive ratios, not absolute differences.
problems.hint: Human hearing is logarithmic. Companding allocates bits where perception is most sensitive.

problems.q: Signal power = 100 W, noise power = 10 W. Calculate SNR in dB.
problems.a:
This is a **power ratio**, so use 10:

$SNR = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{100}{10}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(10) = 10 \times 1 = \mathbf{10 \text{ dB}}$

problems.q: Noise voltage is greater than signal voltage — ratio is 2:1 (noise:signal). What is the SNR in dB?
problems.a:
Signal:Noise voltage ratio = 1:2 = 0.5

$SNR = 20 \log_{10}(0.5) = 20 \times (-0.301) = \mathbf{-6.02 \text{ dB}}$

Negative dB means noise > signal — very poor quality!
problems.hint: If noise > signal, the ratio < 1 → log is negative → negative dB.

problems.q: A system uses 12-bit quantization. You upgrade to 13-bit. How much does SQNR improve?
problems.a:
Each bit adds ~6 dB. Adding 1 bit:

$\Delta SQNR = 6.02 \times 1 = \mathbf{6.02 \text{ dB improvement}}$

12-bit SQNR: $6.02 \times 12 = 72.24$ dB

13-bit SQNR: $6.02 \times 13 = 78.26$ dB
problems.hint: +1 bit always adds ~6 dB, regardless of starting depth.

problems.q: Compare a 10-bit system vs a 12-bit system. What is the SQNR of each, and what is the difference?
problems.a:
**10-bit:** $6.02 \times 10 = 60.2$ dB

**12-bit:** $6.02 \times 12 = 72.24$ dB

**Difference:** $72.24 - 60.2 = \mathbf{12.04 \text{ dB}}$

The 12-bit system has 12 dB better SQNR (2 extra bits × 6 dB each).
